originalTitle: 'Ebola has tortured us': Fear grips eastern DR Congo as deadly virus spreads
title: Hello from Africa! Doctors Are Helping Friends in Congo!
content:
Hello, My Amazing Friends!

Hi everyone! It’s your friend, Kiki the Reporter, here! I'm sending you big smiles and warm greetings from our incredible continent, Africa! Today, I have a very important story to share with you from a beautiful country called DR Congo. It’s a story about brave doctors, kind people, and how everyone is working together to stay healthy and happy.

What is a Tiny Germ Called Ebola?

Sometimes, very, very tiny things called germs can make us feel sick. You can't even see them with your eyes because they are so small! One of these tiny germs is called Ebola. When someone gets this germ, they might feel hot, like when you have a fever. They might feel very tired or have a tummy ache. It’s like when you have the flu, but a different kind of germ. Did you know? Our bodies are amazing! They have tiny superhero cells inside that fight off germs to keep us healthy!

Where is This Happening?

DR Congo is a big, beautiful country right here in Africa! It has amazing green forests, big rivers, and lots of friendly people. Imagine a place with lots of sunshine and sometimes even special animals like chimpanzees and gorillas living in its wild parts! This tiny Ebola germ has been making some people feel sick in the eastern part of DR Congo. But don't worry! There are lots of clever and kind doctors and nurses, like real-life superheroes, who are there to help everyone get better. Did you know? DR Congo is home to the okapi, a very shy animal that looks a bit like a zebra and a giraffe combined!

Our Doctors Are Superheroes!

When people get sick, doctors and nurses are the first ones to rush and help. They are like champions who wear special clothes to keep themselves safe. They work super hard to give people medicine that helps them feel better. They also make sure sick people drink lots of water and get plenty of rest. Their job is to chase away the tiny germs and help everyone become strong again. At first, it was a little bit tricky for the doctors to find everyone who was sick. It was like the tiny germ was playing hide-and-seek! They said it was a bit slow to find all the people. But guess what? Now, the doctors are super-fast! They are 'playing catch-up' with the germ, which means they are finding sick people quickly and helping them right away. They are so smart and good at their job!

How Can We Stay Safe from Germs?

Staying healthy is like having a superpower! And the best superpower is... washing your hands! * **Wash Your Hands!** This is super important! Use soap and water, and rub your hands all over, like you're giving them a bubbly bath. Wash for as long as it takes to sing 'Happy Birthday' twice! This washes away all those tiny, unseen germs. * **Sneeze into Your Elbow!** If you need to sneeze, do it into your elbow, like a little shield! This stops germs from flying into the air. * **Don't Touch Your Face!** Try not to touch your eyes, nose, or mouth with your hands. Germs love to sneak into our bodies through these places! * **Listen to Grown-ups!** Your parents, teachers, and guardians know the best ways to keep you safe and healthy. Always listen to their advice! Did you know? Doctors and scientists work together to make special medicines called vaccines that can teach our bodies how to fight off certain germs before we even get sick!

Being Kind and Caring

When someone is sick, it's very important to be kind and caring. We might not be able to give them a hug if they have a germ like Ebola, because we don't want to get sick too. But we can think good thoughts for them. We can send them happy wishes in our hearts and hope they get better very soon. Being kind makes everyone feel a little bit stronger. Remember, the people who are sick need our kindness and understanding.

Working Together as a Team!

Everyone in DR Congo is working together like a big, amazing team! Families are listening to what the doctors say. Communities are helping each other keep their homes and villages clean and safe. When we all work together, we can make a huge difference! It's like everyone is helping the doctors become even bigger superheroes! Did you know? In Africa, there are many different languages spoken, but people always find ways to understand each other and work together, just like our doctors and communities are doing!

We Are Strong!

Africa is strong, and the people in DR Congo are strong too! With the help of all the brave doctors, nurses, and friends from all over the world, they will make sure this tiny germ doesn't cause too much trouble. They will keep working hard until everyone is healthy and happy again. So, remember my friends: wash your hands, listen to your grown-ups, and always be kind. That's how we stay healthy and make the world a better place! See you next time, my little friends! Keep shining bright!
summary: Doctors and communities in eastern DR Congo are working hard like superheroes to help people get better from a tiny germ called Ebola and are now finding sick people quickly to help them.
whyMatters: This is important because when people get sick, especially from a tiny germ like Ebola, doctors need to help them quickly so they can get well. By working together, washing hands, and listening to grown-ups, everyone can stay healthy and safe, which makes communities strong and happy.

originalTitle: Ghana to face Ivory Coast in Afcon 2027 qualifying
title: Ghana to face Ivory Coast in Afcon 2027 qualifying
content: Ghana will face fellow West African heavyweights Ivory Coast in qualifying for the 2027 Africa Cup of Nations, which will be co-hosted by Kenya, Tanzania and Uganda.

originalTitle: Arsenal win Premier League as Man City held at Bournemouth
title: Arsenal win Premier League as Man City held at Bournemouth
content: Arsenal are crowned champions of the Premier League for the first time in 22 years after Manchester City are held to a 1-1 draw at Bournemouth at the Vitality Stadium, a result which secures European football for the home side for the first time in their history.
